tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.details:
Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.
  • __clang_call_terminate: 1 occurrences<>
  • __kmpc_barrier@plt: 2 occurrences<>
  • __kmpc_end_master@plt: 2 occurrences<>

hint.title: CALL instructions

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
  • VZEROUPPER: 1 occurrences<>
hint.title: Complex instructions

hint.workaround: Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.
hint.title: Conversion instructions
Type of elements and instruction set
hint.txt: 6 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).

hint.title: Matching between your function (in the source code) and the binary function
hint.txt:
The binary function is composed of 6 FP arithmetical operations:
  • 2: addition or subtraction
  • 2: multiply
  • 1: divide
  • 1: square root
The binary function is loading 166 bytes (20 double precision FP elements). The binary function is storing 136 bytes (17 double precision FP elements).

hint.title: Arithmetic intensity
hint.txt: Arithmetic intensity is 0.02 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 29.67 cycles. At this rate:
  • 2% of peak load performance is reached (5.60 out of 192.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 3% of peak store performance is reached (4.58 out of 128.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks
expert.txt:
Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.67 to 22.50 cycles (1.32x speedup).

0% of peak computational performance is used (0.20 out of 24.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
• Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.67 to 8.67 cycles (3.42x speedup).

gain.workaround:
• Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Make array accesses unit-stride:
    • If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA): for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)
gain.details:
Store and arithmetical SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized function can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your function is probably not vectorized.
Only 18% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.67 to 4.25 cycles (6.98x speedup).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

potential.workaround:
• Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
    • use march=native
  • Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible. For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD). However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).
potential.title: FMA
potential.txt: Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.

hint.details:
Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.
  • __clang_call_terminate: 1 occurrences<>
  • __kmpc_barrier@plt: 2 occurrences<>
  • __kmpc_end_master@plt: 2 occurrences<>

hint.title: CALL instructions

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
  • VZEROUPPER: 1 occurrences<>
hint.title: Complex instructions

hint.workaround: Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 29.67 cycles. At this rate:
  • 2% of peak load performance is reached (5.60 out of 192.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 3% of peak store performance is reached (4.58 out of 128.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks
expert.txt:
Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.67 to 22.50 cycles (1.32x speedup).

gain.workaround:
• Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.67 to 8.67 cycles (3.42x speedup).

gain.workaround:
• Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Make array accesses unit-stride:
    • If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA): for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)
gain.details:
Store and arithmetical SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized function can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your function is probably not vectorized.
Only 18% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.67 to 4.25 cycles (6.98x speedup).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

potential.workaround:
• Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
    • use march=native
  • Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible. For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD). However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).
potential.title: FMA
potential.txt: Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
